file.content:
if test -x "${ZRBOB_POST_CHARGE_HOOK}"; then
    buc_step "Running post-charge hook: ${ZRBOB_POST_CHARGE_HOOK}"
    "${ZRBOB_POST_CHARGE_HOOK}" || buc_die "Post-charge hook failed: ${ZRBOB_POST_CHARGE_HOOK}"
  fi
}

# Check whether the crucible is charged (compose project has running containers).
# BCG predicate: returns 0 if charged, 1 if not. Never dies, no output.
rbob_charged_predicate() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" compose -p "${ZRBOB_PROJECT}" ps -q --status running 2>/dev/null | grep -q .
}

# Stop the crucible via compose
rbob_quench() {
  zrbob_sentinel

  buc_step "Stopping crucible: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"

  zrbob_compose --profile sessile down --remove-orphans

  buc_step "Crucible stopped: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"
}

# Hail sentry — call out to the guard (interactive shell)
rbob_hail() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Hailing sentry: ${ZRBOB_SENTRY}"
  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -it "${ZRBOB_SENTRY}" /bin/bash
}

# SSH into bottle — proper terminal session via sentry DNAT
rbob_ssh() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  local z_ssh_user="${ZRBOB_BOTTLE_USER:-root}"
  buc_step "SSH to bottle via port ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION} as ${z_ssh_user}"
  exec ssh -t -p "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
    -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null \
    "${z_ssh_user}@localhost"
}

# Rack bottle — compel the demon to reveal state (interactive shell)
rbob_rack() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Racking bottle: ${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}"
  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -it "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" /bin/bash
}

# Writ sentry — non-interactive command execution in sentry
rbob_writ() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Writ to sentry: ${ZRBOB_SENTRY}"
  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec "${ZRBOB_SENTRY}" "$@"
}

489:  # --wait blocks until all health checks pass (sentry → pentacle → bottle chain)

---

# Build and execute a compose command with standard args
# Usage: zrbob_compose <compose_subcommand> [args...]
zrbob_compose() {
  zrbob_sentinel

  local z_args=()
  z_args+=("compose")
  z_args+=("--env-file" "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRR}")
  z_args+=("--env-file" "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBDC}")
  z_args+=("--env-file" "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBJE}")
  z_args+=("--env-file" "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN}")
--
# Start the crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle) via compose
# Requires: RBOB kindled (which requires RBRN and RBRR)
rbob_charge() {
  zrbob_sentinel

  buc_step "Starting crucible: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"

  # Gate: nameplate must have no uncommitted changes
  if ! git diff --quiet -- "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN}" 2>/dev/null; then
    buc_die "Nameplate has uncommitted changes: ${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN} — commit before charging"
  fi
--
  # Tear down any prior state (tolerates missing project)
  buc_step "Cleaning up any prior state (quenching any preexisting can take a couple of minutes)"
  zrbob_compose --profile sessile down --remove-orphans 2>/dev/null || true

  # Charge invariant: at most one crucible per nameplate per system. The
  # collision scan refuses on any foreign compose project whose network
  # bears our moniker (catches pre-prefix-era residue independent of the
  # current run's project name); the subnet scan catches non-compose
  # networks that bypass label-based detection.
  zrbob_detect_nameplate_collision
  zrbob_detect_subnet_conflict
--
  # --wait blocks until all health checks pass (sentry → pentacle → bottle chain)
  buc_step "Starting services via compose"
  zrbob_compose --profile sessile up -d --wait

  if ! git diff --quiet -- "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN}" 2>/dev/null; then
    buc_die "Nameplate has uncommitted changes: ${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN} — commit before charging"
  fi
--
  # Tear down any prior state (tolerates missing project)
  buc_step "Cleaning up any prior state (quenching any preexisting can take a couple of minutes)"
  zrbob_compose --profile sessile down --remove-orphans 2>/dev/null || true

  # Charge invariant: at most one crucible per nameplate per system. The
  # collision scan refuses on any foreign compose project whose network
  # bears our moniker (catches pre-prefix-era residue independent of the
  # current run's project name); the subnet scan catches non-compose

  zrbob_detect_nameplate_collision

--

  buc_step "Starting services via compose"
  zrbob_compose --profile sessile up -d --wait

aiTitle: Find memo on default CPU count failure

content.text:
Answering both:

## 1. Depot levy timing

From the verification trial (`temp-20260518-180410-486712-986`):

```
canonical-establish started:  18:14:20
rbtdrk_depot_levy PASSED:     18:20:49
total levy:                   6 min 29 sec
```

Of which the probe section:

```
Tether probe:  createTime 18:17:33 → startTime 18:18:15 (43 s queue) → finishTime 18:18:42 (27 s run)
Airgap probe:  createTime 18:18:44 → startTime 18:19:24 (40 s queue) → finishTime 18:19:59 (35 s run)
Probe section total: 18:17:33 → 18:19:59 = 2 min 26 sec
```

The key empirical win: **airgap probe queued only 40 seconds**, not 19 minutes. Serialized, the airgap pool faces zero contention — its slot opens immediately after the tether probe vacates. So the actual cost vs the old fire-and-forget levy is only about `+2-3 minutes`, not `+15-20 minutes` as I projected in the memo. The memo's worst-case estimate was overcautious.

(Both probes terminated with `FAILURE`, expected — the egress-posture assertion is designed to fail in the fresh-levy regime; the framework tolerates any terminal state.)

## 2. Moriah: yes, preventable

Looked at the predicate. The verify-active check at `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:133` invokes `tt/rbw-cic.CrucibleIsCharged.sh`, which routes to `rbob_charged_predicate` in `rbob_bottle.sh:508-511`:

```bash
rbob_charged_predicate() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" compose -p "${ZRBOB_PROJECT}" ps -q --status running 2>/dev/null | grep -q .
}
```

A single shot at the docker daemon: list compose containers in `running` status, grep for any line. **No retry, no timeout, no error distinction.** `2>/dev/null` swallows any daemon error — if the daemon timed out or hiccupped, the predicate quietly returns "not running" indistinguishably from "actually no containers."

That's the failure window. Charge itself uses `docker compose up -d --wait` (rbob_bottle.sh:491), which blocks until health checks pass, so containers *were* running when charge returned. Between charge return and the predicate query, one of:

- **Docker daemon transient hiccup.** Moriah ran fourth in the gauntlet after tadmor's 59 attack cases churned heavy network/iptables state. The daemon is busy reaping. A single ps query in that moment can fail or return empty.
- **A container died right after `--wait` succeeded.** Less likely; would have surfaced on retry too if it were a real bug.

Calibrated honesty: I don't have certainty on which. I have the verify-active failure log and the lack of any container-level evidence in the trace (no compose logs captured). Daemon hiccup is the leading hypothesis because retry passed cleanly and no compose layer change happened between trials.

### Better engineering — three escalating fixes

**Cheapest (recommended) — retry loop in `rbob_charged_predicate`:**

```bash
rbob_charged_predicate() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  local i
  for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
    "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" compose -p "${ZRBOB_PROJECT}" ps -q --status running 2>/dev/null | grep -q . && return 0
    sleep 1
  done
  return 1
}
```

5 tries with 1 s sleeps between. Defensive against transients, no semantic change. ~6 lines of bash.

**Slightly more careful — distinguish daemon failure from genuine no-containers:**

Capture stderr to a temp file, check the exit status of compose itself. If compose exited non-zero (daemon problem), surface the error to the caller. Only treat "compose succeeded but listed zero containers" as a real "not charged" answer.

**Highest-confidence — check named services explicitly:**

Rather than asking "any container running," ask "are sentry, pentacle, and bottle all running by their compose service names?" That distinguishes "all healthy" from "two are healthy, one is restarting" — gives a sharper diagnostic when something is genuinely wrong, not just transient.

My recommendation: **start with cheapest.** The retry loop catches the transient class without changing the predicate's intent or surface area. If post-deployment we see persistent failures *despite* the retry, escalate to option two or three. Slate it as a small pace under ₣BO — maybe 15 minutes of work plus a tP rerun to confirm.

# Tether posture: public reachable AND Google reachable.
  local -r z_tether_egress_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_probe_tether_egress.sh"
  printf '%s\n' \
    '#!/bin/bash' \
    'set -euo pipefail' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://example.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "tether: public reachable OK"' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "tether: google reachable OK"' \
    > "${z_tether_egress_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write tether egress script"

  # Airgap posture: public BLOCKED AND Google reachable. Public-reachable is
  # a hard infra-drift signal — curl's connection error surfaces in the build
  # log as the assertion fails.
  local -r z_airgap_egress_file="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_probe_airgap_egress.sh"
  printf '%s\n' \
    '#!/bin/bash' \
    'set -euo pipefail' \
    'if curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://example.com > /dev/null; then' \
    '  echo "airgap: ERROR public is reachable (expected blocked)" >&2' \
    '  exit 1' \
    'fi' \
    'echo "airgap: public blocked OK"' \
    'curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com > /dev/null' \
    'echo "airgap: google reachable OK"' \
    > "${z_airgap_egress_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write airgap egress script"

  zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "tether" "${z_tether_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_tether_egress_file}"
  zrbgp_pool_probe_submit "${z_token}" "airgap" "${z_airgap_id}" "${z_mason_sa_email}" "${z_airgap_egress_file}"

  buc_step 'Update depot tracking'
  zrbgp_depot_list_update || buc_die "Failed to update depot tracking after creation"

content.text:
Pace `₢BOAAO` (named-service-charge-predicate) slated.

## Why FAILURE is "expected" — and why that's actually a bug

Honest answer: **it isn't expected by design. The probe assertions are broken.** I've been parroting the framework's "tolerated" wording without questioning it. You're right to push.

Step-level evidence from the tether probe build I just pulled:

```json
"failureInfo": {
  "type": "USER_BUILD_STEP",
  "detail": "Build step failure: build step 0 \"...cloud-sdk:alpine\" failed: step exited with non-zero status: 22"
}
```

Exit 22 is **curl's "HTTP error response received"** code. The tether egress script (`rbgp_Payor.sh:1101-1109`) runs two curl checks:

```bash
curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://example.com > /dev/null     # OK — returns 200
curl -fsS --max-time 10 https://storage.googleapis.com > /dev/null   # FAILS — returns 400
```

`https://storage.googleapis.com` (root path) returns **HTTP 400 Bad Request** because the root path requires a bucket or API path. `curl -f` treats that as failure → exit 22 → step fails → build status `FAILURE`.

So the "egress posture assertion" hasn't been asserting anything meaningful since the day it was written. It checks `example.com` successfully, then trips on a malformed GCS probe URL. The framework's tolerance of any terminal state was originally intended for the HTTP 400 *submission* side-effect (quota row materialization on fresh levy) — but it accidentally covers up this real bug in the *build* steps too.

The original commit message's "asserts intended egress posture in-build" is true on paper but false in practice. The probe is currently a no-op for its stated purpose: it doesn't validate egress posture and never reaches step 2 (the `rbi_df` marker push) either, because step 1 fails first.

So three of the probe's three documented purposes:

| Purpose | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| Materialize quota row | Works (HTTP 400 at submission, before any build runs) |
| Assert egress posture | **Broken — bad URL fails step 1, never validates anything** |
| Push `rbi_df` marker | **Broken — step 2 (push) never runs because step 1 fails** |

That's another slate-able pace under ₣BO. Repair candidates for the egress check:

- Replace `https://storage.googleapis.com` with a URL that returns 2xx without auth (e.g., `https://storage.googleapis.com/storage/v1/b/foo` returns 401, still 4xx — that doesn't help; or pick something like `https://storage.googleapis.com/storage/v1/projects/_/serviceAccount` which requires auth too...). Honestly, a healthy GCS endpoint that returns 200 anonymously is rare. Use `--write-out '%{http_code}'` and accept 200|401|403 as "reachable," only fail on connection-refused / timeout. That tests *connectivity*, not authorization.
- For airgap, same logic plus the inversion test.

Want me to slate that as a sibling pace (call it `probe-egress-assertion-repair`), or save it for later? It's small but needs its own discussion because the URL/code-set choice has design tradeoffs.

Mechanical implementation with discipline-reading and spec-update prerequisites.

## Goal
Repair the tether and airgap egress assertion scripts in
`rbgp_Payor.sh` (~lines 1101-1127) so the probes actually validate
egress posture. Currently both probes fail at step 0 because
`curl -fsS https://storage.googleapis.com` exits 22 (HTTP 400 on root
path); the failure has been silently tolerated by the framework's
any-terminal-state policy, defeating the probe's stated purpose and
preventing step 2 (the `rbi_df` marker push) from ever running.

  semantics. The spec currently describes the assertion in idealized
  terms; new text must accurately describe what the assertion tests
  (likely connectivity-vs-authorization distinction).

normal conditions; the `rbi_df` marker reaches GAR (step 2 runs to
completion). Egress assertion distinguishes reachability from
authorization and produces a clear diagnostic when posture is
actually wrong. Spec updated. A levy fixture trial confirms.

content.text:
Direct answers:

**1. Did we wrap?** No. No `jjx_close` call this session.

**2. Is that appropriate?** Strictly yes — *because no pace was ever mounted*. All the work in this session was heat-affiliated to ₣BO, not pace-affiliated. There was nothing to wrap.

**3. But — we probably should have had a pace.** Looking back honestly, the scope here (diagnose + memo + revert experiment + anchor test + repair + spec update + verification) is exactly the size of work that earns a pace. We slid into it because the session started as research ("find the memo") and grew organically into substantive code work. At each step, heat-affiliation was acceptable. In retrospect, it would have been cleaner to slate something like `diagnose-default-cpu-wedge` early on, mount it, and wrap at the end.

The cost of not doing that: `jjx_log` won't surface this session's work as a discrete pace; it'll show up only as a cluster of heat-affiliated commits + the memo. Git log captures everything, the memo captures the diagnosis durably — so nothing is *lost*. It's a discoverability/tidiness shortfall, not a correctness one.

**Should we complete something first?**

One option worth considering before closing: **retroactive slate + immediate wrap.** It would feel a bit ceremonial, but it gives this work a coronet so future `jjx_log` queries can find it cleanly. Something like:

- `jjx_enroll` with `{firemark: "BO", first: true}` → docket explaining what was done
- `jjx_close` with summary

That would put it at the front of the heat (where work that already landed belongs, logically) and mark it closed. If you'd like, I can do that before we end — say the word and I'll write the docket + summary.

Otherwise, the state is clean. Two slated follow-ons (₢BOAAO, ₢BOAAP). All commits pushed. No background tasks. Nothing dangling locally.

message.content: what is the definition of 'cantle'?

content.text:
Per JJK's verb table in CLAUDE.md:

> **cantle** | pace | `jjx_enroll {after: <current_coronet>}`

So "cantle" = enroll a new pace **immediately after the current (mounted) pace** in the heat's ordering. The horse-vocabulary metaphor fits: the cantle is the raised back of a saddle, just behind where the rider sits — i.e., the position immediately following the current one.

Now I owe you a wrinkle: **I didn't strictly cantle.** No pace was mounted in this session, so "the current coronet" was undefined. When you said "cantle this new pace," I interpreted it as "insert after the one we just slated (`₢BOAAO`)" and passed `after: "BOAAO"` to `jjx_enroll`. That produced the ordering you probably wanted (AAO then AAP), but the verb strictly required a mounted pace I didn't have.

Two cleaner readings of what you may have meant:

1. **You meant "slate it, but specifically positioned after AAO" — and used "cantle" loosely.** That's what I delivered. AAP now sits immediately after AAO in the heat.
2. **You meant "literally cantle the verb," implying I should have mounted something first.** In which case I executed under a stretched definition.

If (2) is the right read, the fix is either to retroactively mount one of the new paces and let the cantle stand semantically, or to just acknowledge the verb-stretch and move on. Operationally both AAO and AAP are in the right place in the heat order regardless.
